title: Deepening Hidden Representations from Pre-trained Language Models

structured.summary: The paper introduces the HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) to improve Transformer-based model performance by leveraging hidden layer representations. Evaluated on the GLUE benchmark, HIRE outperforms the baseline RoBERTa model on several tasks, achieving competitive results without altering the core model architecture.

strengths.point: Innovative use of hidden layer representations.
strengths.evidence: The HIRE component dynamically captures information from multiple layers, enhancing the existing model performance as shown by improvements on GLUE benchmark tasks.
The approach does not alter the core architecture or training objectives of the RoBERTa model, showing the adaptability of the enhancement.

weaknesses.point: Lack of novelty in using hidden layers.
weaknesses.evidence: The idea of leveraging information from hidden layers has been explored in earlier works, such as those by Devlin et al. (2019) and Peters et al. (2018b).

weaknesses.point: Methodology lacks exploration of alternative fusion approaches.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper focuses solely on a two-layer bidirectional GRU for fusion without considering other methods like attention mechanisms.
Results are mostly compared only to the RoBERTa model, not against various competing models implementing similar enhancements.
The description of the HIRE and Fusion Network components lacks depth, particularly in explaining the choice and implementation of importance scores and fusion strategy.
The paper does not provide key hyperparameter settings or specific training processes for reproducibility.

questions.question: How does the computational overhead of implementing HIRE compare to its performance gains?
questions.motivation: Understanding the trade-offs in computational costs can guide real-world application viability and optimization.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty in using hidden layers.. The idea of leveraging information from hidden layers has been explored in earlier works, such as those by Devlin et al. (2019) and Peters et al. (2018b)
critique_points.point_002: Methodology lacks exploration of alternative fusion approaches.. The paper focuses solely on a two-layer bidirectional GRU for fusion without considering other methods like attention mechanisms
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation against other models.. Results are mostly compared only to the RoBERTa model, not against various competing models implementing similar enhancements
critique_points.point_004: Clarity issues in component explanation.. The description of the HIRE and Fusion Network components lacks depth, particularly in explaining the choice and implementation of importance scores and fusion strategy
critique_points.point_005: Insufficient reproducibility details.. The paper does not provide key hyperparameter settings or specific training processes for reproducibility
